clue: On a November night, jubilant crowds dismantled a concrete barrier that had divided a once-great central European capital for nearly three decades, as the guards on both sides stood down and families reunited for the first time in a generation.

locationName: Latin Bridge, Sarajevo, Bosnia and Herzegovina
clue: In a narrow street beside a river bridge in an old Austro-Hungarian provincial capital, a young nationalist fired the shots that would ignite a continental war and redraw the map of an entire empire.

On the chalky ridge of a southern island coast, a duke from across a narrow channel routed the last of a line of island-born kings, forever altering the language, law, and nobility of the realm he had seized.

Beside a great natural harbour crowned by an arching steel span, a revolutionary concert hall of interlocking shell vaults opened its doors after more than a decade of cost overruns, political scandal, and the resignation of its visionary architect.

On a clear summer morning over a mid-sized port city spread across river delta islands, a single aircraft released a weapon of unprecedented destructive force, instantly incinerating the city centre and inaugurating the nuclear age.

locationName: Place de la Bastille, Paris, France
clue: On a sweltering July afternoon, tradespeople and mutinous soldiers stormed a medieval fortress-prison that had stood as a symbol of royal authority for centuries, seizing its small garrison and few prisoners, and touching off a revolution that would reshape the western world.

locationName: Kill Devil Hills, North Carolina, USA
clue: On the wind-scoured dunes of a barrier island along the eastern seaboard of a young republic, two brothers who built and repaired bicycles for a living achieved twelve seconds of sustained, controlled, powered flight in a craft heavier than air.

locationName: Largo di Torre Argentina, Rome, Italy
clue: On the Ides of March, in a colonnaded theatre complex that also served as a meeting hall for the ancient world's most powerful deliberative body, a coalition of senators surrounded the republic's most formidable general and struck him down with concealed blades.

In the early hours of an April morning, a safety test at a graphite-moderated reactor in a vast empire's northern territory spiralled out of control, triggering a meltdown that scattered radioactive material across the continent and forced the permanent evacuation of a nearby planned city.

High above a winding river gorge in the cloud-draped mountains of a great sun-worshipping empire, stonemasons fitted massive granite blocks without mortar to build a royal retreat that would be abandoned within a century and remain unknown to the outside world for three hundred years.
